verses.text: Moses said: I am 120 years old; I can no more go out and come in. The LORD hath said, Thou shalt not go over this Jordan. The LORD thy God Himself will go over before thee. Joshua shall go before thee. Be strong and of a good courage, fear not: for the LORD thy God goes with thee; He will not fail thee, nor forsake thee. Guidance: 'He will not fail nor forsake' — the sentence Hebrews 13 will quote at every reader's bedside. Moses cannot cross, but God will. The leader's absence is not the end of the people; the LORD's presence is. Courage is not a feeling to muster; it is a verdict on whose company you keep.

Moses called unto Joshua, said in the sight of all Israel: Be strong and of a good courage: for thou must go with this people unto the land which the LORD hath sworn; thou shalt cause them to inherit it. The LORD, He it is that doth go before thee; He will be with thee, He will not fail thee, neither forsake thee: fear not, neither be dismayed. Guidance: 'Be strong and of a good courage' — said to Israel, then to Joshua personally. The transfer was public. Joshua hears in front of everyone what God will do for him. Sometimes calling needs witnesses; the new leader needs the room to remember the moment too.

Moses wrote this law, delivered it unto the priests who bear the ark, and unto all the elders. At the end of every seven years, in the feast of tabernacles, thou shalt read this law before all Israel in their hearing. Gather the people: men, women, children, the stranger within thy gates, that they may hear, learn, fear the LORD, observe to do all the words. That their children, which have not known, may hear and learn. Guidance: Every seven years, the whole text read aloud to the whole nation — including immigrants and children too small to understand. The next generation hears the words BEFORE they understand them. Repetition is the soil that grows comprehension. The law is not just learned; it is broadcast.

The LORD said unto Moses: thy days approach that thou must die. Moses and Joshua came; the LORD appeared in the tabernacle in a pillar of a cloud. The LORD: thou shalt sleep with thy fathers; this people will go a whoring after the gods of the strangers, forsake Me, break My covenant. My anger shall be kindled; I will hide My face; they will say, Are not these evils come upon us, because our God is not among us? I will surely hide My face in that day. Guidance: God tells Moses ahead of time exactly what will happen — and gives him a song to write to meet the future. The hiding of the face is the saddest punishment: not the smiting hand, but the turned shoulder. And He warns about it before it lands.

Now write ye this song, teach it the children of Israel, put it in their mouths, that this song may be a witness for Me. When I shall have brought them into the land that floweth with milk and honey; they shall have eaten, waxen fat; then will they turn unto other gods. This song shall testify against them as a witness; it shall not be forgotten out of the mouths of their seed. So Moses wrote this song the same day, taught it the children of Israel. Guidance: God's testimony is set inside the people's mouths. Songs outlive sermons; people sing in exile what they heard in church. The whoredom is coming — but so is the chorus that will remind them what they knew. Music is theology made unforgettable.

He gave Joshua son of Nun a charge, said: Be strong and of a good courage: thou shalt bring the children of Israel into the land which I sware: I will be with thee. Guidance: This time the charge is from God Himself, not Moses. The same words, but with the divine signature. Joshua now has both — the prophet's blessing and the LORD's promise. He will need both, eight chapters from now in the book that bears his name.

When Moses had made an end of writing the words of this law in a book, until they were finished, Moses commanded the Levites: Take this book of the law, put it in the side of the ark of the covenant of the LORD, that it may be there for a witness against thee. For I know thy rebellion, thy stiff neck: behold, while I am yet alive ye have been rebellious; how much more after my death? Guidance: The law goes IN the ark, beside the tablets — a witness to the future. Moses' diagnosis is unsentimental: I know you'll rebel after I'm gone. The book sleeps next to the covenant box, ready to convict. Honest leaders prepare for their absence by setting the words in stone — and parchment.

Gather unto me all the elders of your tribes, that I may speak these words in their ears, call heaven and earth to record against them. For I know that after my death ye will utterly corrupt yourselves, turn aside from the way which I have commanded you; evil will befall you in the latter days. Moses spake in the ears of all the congregation of Israel the words of this song. Guidance: The elders gather; the witnesses are heaven and earth themselves. Then Moses sings — for thirty verses (chapter 32). The song is not optimism; it is preventive medicine. Sing it now so you will remember it later when the prophet is gone.
